title: Team
text:
The SEEC leadership team decided to present our members in three groups: leadership team, rest of the core team and affiliates. We used the below guidelines on what is expected of the different categories of affiliation to decide who should be a core member vs affiliate. This was circulated to the all SEEC members for feedback and adjusted on the website accordingly. The curren team and affiliations can be seen in Table @tab:affaca.
SEEC Academics and Postdoctoral Fellows (Core Team)

These are people who view the success of SEEC and their own academic success as being connected to some degree.
They attend the monthly SEEC staff meetings as this is where the direction of SEEC is developed.
Take a leadership role or assist with the organising of the various facets of SEEC e.g. Stats Toolbox, research seminars.
Make an effort to contribute to raising funds to support SEEC core activities and shared resources.
Benefit from shared SEEC resources (admin support, computers, office space).
Contribute ideas when called upon to do so.
Attend the annual SEEC Student symposium if at all possible.
Make every reasonable effort to attend research meetings.
List SEEC as an affiliation on any papers written or presentations given.
Contribute to the SEEC annual report.
Actively collaborate with other SEEC members.
If not based at a South African University, SEEC core members need to be Honorary Research Associates.
Are on the seec-staff-l mailing list.


SEEC Postgraduates

These are students whose research project and interests fall within the parameters of Statistics in Ecology, Environment and Conservation and whose supervisor is supportive of the connection to the group. They have a (co)supervisor who is part of the core group or an Affiliated Academic.
Present at least two talks each year. One will be at the annual SEEC symposium and the other will be at the research meetings held at three-weekly intervals.
Attend the annual SEEC Student symposium.
Attend all research meetings (arrangements can be made for SEEC students who are not based in Cape Town).
List SEEC as an affiliation on any papers written or presentations given.
Are on the seec-all-l mailing list.

SEEC Affiliated Academic

These academics are SEEC members but not willing or able to contribute to the same degree as a core team member, either because of commitments with other research groups or because their area of interest only overlaps partly with SEEC’s vision.
Add their SEEC affiliation to their publications where appropriate.
Willing to work with the group, contribute to meetings and joint projects, bring opportunities to the attention of the group and promote the group in conversations if the opportunity arises.
Can benefit from shared SEEC resources if they contribute funding.
Postgraduate students of affiliated academics can be SEEC postgraduate students.
Can be SEEC Honorary Research Associates if not based at a South African university.
Attend the SEEC student symposium and research meetings if possible.
Are on the seec-staff-l mailing list.

SEEC Undergraduates

Undergraduate students who are interested in the work we do at SEEC.
They can be involved in research projects.
They are encouraged to participate in the Research Meetings and to attend the student symposium.
They can be mentored by SEEC Postgraduates or academics.
Are on the seec-all-l mailing list.

SEEC Advisory Board

Attend and give inputs at board meetings.
Give inputs and advice when called upon.
Are being updated about SEEC activities through the annual report and at meetings.

SEEC Partners

Academics or scientists working at a university or research organisation who collaborate with the SEEC core team, be it as co-supervisors, or collaborators on projects and funding applications.
These academics are not formally part of the SEEC team and are not expected to list SEEC as an affiliation on papers and presentations.
Can be consulted for inputs on SEEC strategy or projects.
Can be on the the seec-friends-l mailing list.

SEEC Friends

Anyone who is interested in what we do and would like to be informed of seminars (Stats Toolbox) and courses.
There is no obligation or commitment either on part of SEEC or the Friends.
Are on the seec-friends-l mailing list or keep themselves in the loop through the SEEC facebook page, twitter or web site.

title: Home
text:
Last updated: 2026-01-23 09:51 SAST
This site presents the SEEC 2025 Annual Report as a live, web-based document.
All tables are generated directly from the SEEC Google Sheets and update automatically whenever the site is rebuilt.

title: Grants
text: Last updated: 2026-01-21 13:10 SAST

title: SEEC Year in Review
text:
2025



In 2025, SEEC delivered a full programme of training and community activities (symposium, short course, ecological forecasting course, research seminars, writing retreat), while also strengthening organisational foundations through re-accreditation, improved administration capacity, clearer postgraduate support structures, improved financial governance, and more formalised communications and reporting workflows.






27–28 May 2025

SEEC hosted an expanded two-day Symposium on Statistical Ecology & Quantitative Biology at UCT.

Approximately 25 abstracts were received, enabling a full two-day programme.
The symposium included postgraduate student and postdoctoral presentations, with participation from external research groups.
CoE-MASS funding support enabled the expanded format.






21–25 July 2025

SEEC hosted a five-day course at UCT.

Led by Mike Dietze, with contributions from SEEC staff and collaborators.
Teaching resources were made available online after the course for broader access.
Post-course follow-up included reporting to external platforms (e.g. SAEON eNews) and exploration of future Africa-focused forecasting outputs.






27–31 October 2025

SEEC delivered the annual Statistics Short Course (online format).

The course proceeded successfully despite lower-than-usual paid uptake.
Participant feedback was strongly positive (reported as ~90% rating ≥4/5).
Course naming was refined to “Statistics (with R)” to clarify that R supports the course rather than being the primary objective.
Migration of course material from Vula to Amathuba was initiated following Vula’s discontinuation, with initial conversion completed in 2025.






8–11 December 2025

SEEC held a Writing Retreat (3 nights).

Approximately 10 participants, prioritising staff and postgraduate students with active manuscripts or thesis chapters.
Participation criteria were formalised at leadership level to prioritise students nearing completion.
Supported through CoE-MASS funding and aligned with SEEC’s focus on outputs and completion.






11 October 2025

SEEC Pizza Party (informal community event for staff, students, and visitors).









SEEC progressed through a formal review process during 2025 and was re-accredited for a further five years (until end of 2030).
SEEC retained a co-director leadership structure, supporting cross-departmental anchoring.
Leadership engagement with Faculty highlighted challenges in review processes, administration, and cross-departmental positioning.





Nomfundo Hlongwa joined as a shared administrator (SEEC allocation: 20%) from May 2025.
Work continued to clarify core vs affiliated membership expectations and participation norms.
Development of departmental MoUs progressed (starting with Statistical Sciences) to improve continuity and stability across departments.
Leadership discussions explicitly addressed financial sustainability and cost recovery, including engagement at Faculty level on cost-recovery models.





Bi-weekly SEEC research meetings continued through 2025, with renewed emphasis on:

ensuring every student presents at least once annually,
creating more structured scheduling and tracking (e.g. shared schedule/spreadsheet identifying students who have and have not presented),
strengthening proposal presentation expectations for new MSc/PhD students.

Leadership discussed the introduction of PhD advisory committees (beyond supervisors) to support steady progress and accountability.
A consolidated postgraduate starter pack was initiated, collating guidance on:

admin pathways,
literature searching and reading,
writing support,
responsible AI use,
GitHub/version control,
high-performance computing access.






CoE-MASS funding supported SEEC’s major activities (symposium expansion and writing retreat).
SEEC secured a Carnegie DEAL postdoctoral fellowship for 2026, with the award decision confirmed in 2025.

Leadership agreed on flexible allocation depending on outcomes of parallel funding applications.

Funding pipeline development progressed, including:

exploration of major calls (e.g. NRF thematic funding, AI–climate-aligned opportunities, IKI Large Grants),
continued development of applied-partnership funding conversations (e.g. BIRDIE-related discussions),
engagement with UCT’s Development and Alumni Department (DAD) around structured fundraising and research-chair-scale proposals.

Development of a SEEC business plan, aligned with MARIS-style planning, was initiated to support MoUs, major funding applications, and long-term sustainability.





Website and communications responsibilities transitioned during 2025, with stronger emphasis on:

keeping student and staff pages current,
ensuring the publication list is up to date,
improving reporting inputs (spreadsheets) to support semi-automated reporting.

Social media focus increasingly shifted toward LinkedIn and Bluesky, supported by reusable templates for publications, achievements, and announcements.
Better tracking of conference attendance, achievements/prizes, and outputs was introduced to support annual reporting.
Michelle resigned as SEEC communications lead in December 2025 and was replaced by postdoctoral fellow Stephanie, who completed Drupal training and assumed responsibility for website updates and outreach.

title: SEEC Events
text:
27–28 May 2025

Expanded SEEC Symposium on Statistical Ecology & Quantitative Biology, held at UCT.

A two-day event with approximately 25 abstracts, including student presentations, postdoctoral contributions, and invited participants from external research groups.

Supported through CoE-MASS funding and included a plenary address by an external speaker.





21–25 July 2025
- Five-day course hosted at UCT.
- Led by Mike Dietze, with contributions from SEEC staff.
- Course materials were made available online after the event, and the course was reported as successful.




27–31 October 2025
- Annual SEEC Statistics Short Course, delivered fully online.
- Approximately 60 participants registered, and the course proceeded as planned.
- Participant feedback was very positive (around 90% of respondents rated the course ≥4/5).




8–11 December 2025
- SEEC Writing Retreat, held at Grootbos Nature Reserve.
- A three-night retreat with approximately 10 participants, prioritising staff and postgraduate students working on advanced drafts of manuscripts or theses.
- Funded via CoE-MASS.




11 October 2025
- SEEC Pizza Party, an informal social gathering for SEEC staff, students, and visitors.

title: SEEC Year in Review (2025)
text:
Beyond events: organisational progress in 2025

Governance and accreditation

SEEC progressed through a formal review process during 2025 and was re-accredited for a further five years (until end of 2030).
SEEC retained a co-director leadership structure, supporting cross-departmental anchoring.



Administration and operations

Nomfundo Hlongwa joined as a shared administrator (SEEC allocation: 20%) from May 2025, strengthening day-to-day operational support.
Work continued to clarify core vs affiliated membership expectations and participation norms.
Development of departmental MoUs progressed (starting with Statistical Sciences) to improve continuity and stability across departments.



Postgraduate coordination and support

Bi-weekly SEEC research meetings continued through 2025, with renewed emphasis on:

ensuring every student presents at least once annually,
creating more structured scheduling and tracking (e.g., shared schedule/spreadsheet),
strengthening proposal presentation expectations for new MSc/PhD students.

A consolidated postgraduate starter pack was progressed, collating guidance on:

admin pathways,
literature searching and reading,
writing support,
responsible AI use,
GitHub/version control,
high-performance computing access.




Funding pipeline and partnerships

CoE-MASS funding supported SEEC’s major activities (symposium expansion and writing retreat).
SEEC secured a Carnegie DEAL postdoctoral fellowship for 2026 (award decision communicated in 2025).
Funding pipeline development progressed, including major-call exploration (e.g., NRF thematic funding and climate/AI-aligned opportunities) and continued development of applied-partnership funding conversations (e.g., BIRDIE-related discussions).



Communications, reporting, and visibility

Website and communications responsibilities transitioned during 2025, with stronger emphasis on:

keeping student and staff pages current,
ensuring the publication list is up to date,
improving reporting inputs (spreadsheets) to support semi-automated reporting.

Social media focus increasingly shifted toward LinkedIn and Bluesky.
Better tracking of conference attendance, achievements/prizes, and outputs was introduced to support annual reporting.



Teaching infrastructure

Migration planning and implementation began to move short-course content from Vula to Amathuba (prompted by Vula discontinuation), with an emphasis on consolidating materials and enabling reuse.

title: SEEC Year in Review (2025)
text:
2025 highlights at a glance

Accreditation: Re-accredited through 2030

Training: Ecological Forecasting Course (July), Statistics Short Course (Oct), Toolbox seminars resumed

Research support: Writing Retreat (Dec), ongoing research meetings

Visibility: Expanded national-facing symposium (May)

Operations: Shared administrator appointed (May) and reporting/communications strengthened
